reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper studies the learning-based low-rank approximation problem and proposes a new algorithm, which is based on the tucker tensor decomposition. The new optimization problem based on tensor decomposition can be seen as a relaxation of the original LRA problem. The experimental result suggests that the proposed algorithm has a lower error while having a faster training time.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strength

1. The paper proposes an algorithm from a new insight, which has not been considered in the literature on the learning-based LRA. The paper also gives a theoretical guarantee.

2. The experimental results are strong and interesting. The proposed algorithm has a lower error while having a faster training time. 

Weakness

0. Note that the template of this paper is actually a little different than the ICLR template.
1. The analysis of the theorem is straightforward. Besides, the Theorem 2 seems only to give an additive error $\epsilon ||A||_F^2$,  I am wondering that would it be possible to get a multiplicative error $\epsilon ||A- A_k||_F^2$, which is the common setting in the literature of the sketching-based LRA.
2. As mentioned in the paper, the new learning-based sketch matrix is a dense matrix, which I think is the main drawback of this paper. The dense sketch matrix seems not to be useful when the size of the data is large. Note that if $S$ is a Count-Sketch matrix, we can compute $SA$ in $nnz(A)$ time. That is one of the reasons that most of the previous works only study the sparse sketching matrix.  As mentioned in the work of IVY19, One applications scenario of the learning-based sketch involves processing streams of data (video, data logs, customer activity etc) by executing the same algorithm on an hourly, daily or weekly basis. Hence in this sense the testing time is more important if the training time is only a little slower as the training process can be done before the new data comes. Also, notice that the sparse matrix has the pass-efficiency property when the data size exceeds memory available in RAM, which is what the author mentioned in the conclusion section. The update time for one update of the Count-Sketch matrix is $O(1)$.

3. The paper mentions the testing time in the experiment section but it seems not to be done in the correct way. It says in the paper that "have no idea how to accelerate the matrix multiplication SA while exploiting the sparse structure of S". However, suppose that the position and value vectors are $p$, $v$. Note that if the $t$-th non-zero entries of $A$ is $A_{ij} = c$, we just need to do the update 
$
SA[p_i, j] = SA[p_i, j]  + v_i \cdot c . 
$
which means the time to compute $SA$ is the same as to read the matrix $A$. 

4. The authors mention the memory usage in the experiment section. Can the author give a detailed result of the memory usage for all approaches?
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: See the last question for detail.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Overall I think the approach the author proposed is interesting. My main concern is the non-sparse of the sketch matrix so currently I vote as a 5  the paper.  I can raise the score if the author can address this adequately (note that the sparsity property is not the only way to compute $SA$ fast, like the Subsampled Randomized Hadamard Transform(SRHT) matrix is a dense sketching matrix while we can compute $SA$ in $O(nd \log n)$ time) .

Summary Of The Paper:
The paper studies the learning-based low-rank approximation problem and proposes a new algorithm, which is based on the tucker tensor decomposition. The new optimization problem based on tensor decomposition can be seen as a relaxation of the original LRA problem. The experimental result suggests that the proposed algorithm has a lower error while having a faster training time.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strength

1. The paper proposes an algorithm from a new insight, which has not been considered in the literature on the learning-based LRA. The paper also gives a theoretical guarantee.

2. The experimental results are strong and interesting. The proposed algorithm has a lower error while having a faster training time. 

Weakness

1. The analysis of the theorem is straightforward. 2. As mentioned in the paper, the new learning-based sketch matrix is a dense matrix, which I think is the main drawback of this paper. The dense sketch matrix seems not to be useful when the size of the data is large. Note that if $S$ is a Count-Sketch matrix, we can compute $SA$ in $nnz(A)$ time. That is one of the reasons that most of the previous works only study the sparse sketching matrix.  As mentioned in the work of IVY19, One applications scenario of the learning-based sketch involves processing streams of data (video, data logs, customer activity etc) by executing the same algorithm on an hourly, daily or weekly basis. Hence in this sense the testing time is more important if the training time is only a little slower as the training process can be done before the new data comes. Also, notice that the sparse matrix has the pass-efficiency property when the data size exceeds memory available in RAM, which is what the author mentioned in the conclusion section. The update time for one update of the Count-Sketch matrix is $O(1)$.

3. The paper mentions the testing time in the experiment section but it seems not to be done in the correct way. It says in the paper that "have no idea how to accelerate the matrix multiplication SA while exploiting the sparse structure of S". However, suppose that the position and value vectors are $p$, $v$. Note that if the $t$-th non-zero entries of $A$ is $A_{ij} = c$, we just need to do the update 
$

$
which means the time to compute $SA$ is the same as to read the matrix $A$. 

4. The authors mention the memory usage in the experiment section. Can the author give a detailed result of the memory usage for all approaches?

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
See the last question for detail.

Summary Of The Review:
Overall I think the approach the author proposed is interesting. My main concern is the non-sparse of the sketch matrix so currently I vote as a 5  the paper.  I can raise the score if the author can address this adequately (note that the sparsity property is not the only way to compute $SA$ fast, like the Subsampled Randomized Hadamard Transform(SRHT) matrix is a dense sketching matrix while we can compute $SA$ in $O(nd \log n)$ time) .

reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper studies the learning-based low-rank approximation problem and proposes a new algorithm, which is based on the tucker tensor decomposition. The new optimization problem based on tensor decomposition can be seen as a relaxation of the original LRA problem. The experimental result suggests that the proposed algorithm has a lower error while having a faster training time.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strength

1. The paper proposes an algorithm from a new insight, which has not been considered in the literature on the learning-based LRA. The paper also gives a theoretical guarantee.

2. The experimental results are strong and interesting. The proposed algorithm has a lower error while having a faster training time. 

Weakness

0. Note that the template of this paper is actually a little different than the ICLR template.
1. The analysis of the theorem is straightforward. Besides, the Theorem 2 seems only to give an additive error $\epsilon ||A||_F^2$,  I am wondering that would it be possible to get a multiplicative error $\epsilon ||A- A_k||_F^2$, which is the common setting in the literature of the sketching-based LRA.
2. As mentioned in the paper, the new learning-based sketch matrix is a dense matrix, which I think is the main drawback of this paper. The dense sketch matrix seems not to be useful when the size of the data is large. Note that if $S$ is a Count-Sketch matrix, we can compute $SA$ in $nnz(A)$ time. That is one of the reasons that most of the previous works only study the sparse sketching matrix.  As mentioned in the work of IVY19, One applications scenario of the learning-based sketch involves processing streams of data (video, data logs, customer activity etc) by executing the same algorithm on an hourly, daily or weekly basis. Hence in this sense the testing time is more important if the training time is only a little slower as the training process can be done before the new data comes. Also, notice that the sparse matrix has the pass-efficiency property when the data size exceeds memory available in RAM, which is what the author mentioned in the conclusion section. The update time for one update of the Count-Sketch matrix is $O(1)$.

3. The paper mentions the testing time in the experiment section but it seems not to be done in the correct way. It says in the paper that "have no idea how to accelerate the matrix multiplication SA while exploiting the sparse structure of S". However, suppose that the position and value vectors are $p$, $v$. Note that if the $t$-th non-zero entries of $A$ is $A_{ij} = c$, we just need to do the update 
$
SA[p_i, j] = SA[p_i, j]  + v_i \cdot c . 
$
which means the time to compute $SA$ is the same as to read the matrix $A$. 

4. The authors mention the memory usage in the experiment section. Can the author give a detailed result of the memory usage for all approaches?

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
See the last question for detail.

Summary Of The Review:
Overall I think the approach the author proposed is interesting. My main concern is the non-sparse of the sketch matrix so currently I vote as a 5  the paper.  I can raise the score if the author can address this adequately (note that the sparsity property is not the only way to compute $SA$ fast, like the Subsampled Randomized Hadamard Transform(SRHT) matrix is a dense sketching matrix while we can compute $SA$ in $O(nd \log n)$ time) .

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: EAGLE: Large-scale Learning of Turbulent Fluid Dynamics with Mesh Transformers

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper introduces a dataset for predicting fluid turbulences and a method using a Transformer to learn the dynamics. It adds clustering and graph pooling before the attention block so that the receptive field is not constrained in a local neighborhood. Results show that the new method outperforms the previous works.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strength:
1. Although different shapes of the boundary affect how the mesh is constructed and how they are clustered, it seems that the model still generalizes well.
2. The results are promising and supportive of the technical contribution.

Weakness:
1. Missing reference: Takahashi, Tetsuya, et al. "Differentiable fluids with solid coupling for learning and control." Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence. Vol. 35. No. 7. 2021.
2. I wonder how this can be applied to 3D cases. Since the 2D task only there is already 270Gb data, it is probably not practical to prepare a good amount of data for the model to converge and generalize. Also, dealing with tetrahedra instead of triangles may introduce unknown challenges. Please add more insights regarding the extension to 3D in the conclusion section.
3. How does the mesh resolution affect the training/test accuracy? If the training and test images are of different resolutions, will the model perform better or worse? It would be great to see such comparisons in the experiments.

Summary Of The Paper:
This paper introduces a dataset for predicting fluid turbulences and a method using a Transformer to learn the dynamics. It adds clustering and graph pooling before the attention block so that the receptive field is not constrained in a local neighborhood. Strength And Weaknesses:

reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents EAGLE, a large‐scale synthetic dataset comprising over 1.1 million time‐varying 2D irregular meshes that simulate turbulent airflow around a moving UAV over procedurally generated ground geometries. Building on this benchmark, the authors propose a mesh transformer architecture that first applies offline geometric clustering and learned graph pooling, then performs multi‐head self‐attention on the coarsened tokens, and finally decodes back to the original mesh resolution with a GNN decoder. Comprehensive experiments on EAGLE and two established benchmarks (Cylinder‐Flow, Scalar‐Flow) demonstrate state‐of‐the‐art forecasting accuracy, and extensive ablations confirm the benefits of global, adaptive attention, clustering strategies, and generalization to unseen geometries.

Strengths  
1. Dataset scale and diversity.  EAGLE fills an important gap by providing an unprecedented volume of 2D turbulent‐flow data on irregular meshes, with dynamic remeshing that tracks a moving source and 600 distinct boundary profiles. This resource will be valuable to the fluid‐dynamics and geometric‐deep‐learning communities.  
2. Methodological innovation.  The mesh transformer’s combination of offline clustering, graph‐level pooling, and single‐hop global self‐attention efficiently captures long‐range dependencies, outperforming strong GNN baselines (MeshGraphNet, GAT) and grid‐based CNNs (DilResNet) on multiple roll‐out horizons.  
3. Thorough evaluation and analysis.  The paper presents quantitative SOTA results, qualitative visualizations of attention maps aligned with flow structures, and ablations on cluster size, attention scope, and generalization to new geometries and mesh resolutions. Failure‐case discussions and comparisons against prior work on irregular meshes reinforce the technical contributions.

Weaknesses  
1. Missing prior work citation.  The manuscript does not reference Takahashi et al. (AAAI 2021), “Differentiable fluids with solid coupling for learning and control,” which directly relates to learned fluid‐dynamics models with solid boundaries.  
2. Limited discussion of 3D extension.  While the 2D scope is justified by data‐scale considerations (270 GB for 2D alone), real‐world applications demand 3D simulations with tetrahedral meshes. The paper should provide insights or preliminary discussion on memory/computational scaling, possible clustering strategies in 3D, and expected challenges.  
3. Compute and reproducibility concerns.  Generating and training on such a large dataset requires substantial CFD solver resources and high‐end hardware, which may hinder adoption and replication. Certain mesh‐downsampling heuristics, solver configurations, and hyperparameters (e.g., cluster sizes, attention heads) are relegated to the appendix, making it harder for practitioners to reproduce the results without extensive effort.  
4. Mesh‐resolution generalization.  Although generalization to unseen mesh resolutions is showcased, it remains unclear how training/test mismatches in resolution quantitatively affect performance. Explicit experiments comparing accuracy when training and inference resolutions differ would strengthen claims of robustness.  
5. Trajectory and error accumulation.  The UAV follows a single canonical path in all simulations, limiting diversity of source motions. Moreover, like many autoregressive models, the transformer exhibits error accumulation over long rollouts; a more detailed breakdown of this phenomenon and mitigation strategies (e.g., scheduled sampling) would be beneficial.

Suggestions for Improvement  
– Add the missing reference to Takahashi et al. (AAAI 2021) and discuss how the proposed mesh transformer complements or differs from differentiable‐PDE‐solver approaches with solid coupling.  
– In the conclusion or a dedicated discussion section, outline pathways to 3D extension: anticipated data‐volume trade-offs, clustering on tetrahedral meshes, and possible architectural adaptations.  
– Integrate key implementation details (mesh‐downsampling rules, solver settings, clustering hyperparameters) into the main text or a README to lower the barrier for replication. Consider providing a lightweight version of the dataset or model for benchmarking on modest hardware.  
– Include experiments showing performance sensitivity to training/inference mesh‐resolution mismatches, and report error accumulation trends over extended autoregressive rollouts.  
– Discuss the fixed UAV trajectory setup and, if possible, explore simple variations to demonstrate model robustness to different motion patterns.

Decision  
Rating: 6 (marginally above the acceptance threshold)  
I recommend a weak reject in its current form due to the missing reference, limited 3D discussion, and reproducibility concerns. However, I am open to elevating my recommendation if the authors address these issues in their rebuttal.

6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper  
This paper tackles the problem of learning to forecast 2D turbulent fluid dynamics on irregular meshes induced by a single moving flow source. Building on an autoregressive graph-based framework, the authors introduce a novel “mesh transformer” architecture that (1) performs offline geometric clustering to coarsen the mesh, (2) pools node features into cluster embeddings, (3) applies multi‐head self-attention on the reduced token set to capture long-range dependencies in one shot, and (4) decodes back to the original resolution via a GNN decoder. To evaluate this approach, the authors assemble a large‐scale synthetic benchmark—dubbed EAGLE—comprised of roughly 1.1 million time‐varying 2D meshes simulating unsteady, turbulent airflow around a moving UAV over procedurally generated ground geometries. The proposed method outperforms strong GNN and grid‐CNN baselines on EAGLE as well as on standard Cylinder-Flow and Scalar-Flow benchmarks, and the paper provides ablations on clustering, receptive fields, and generalization.

Strengths  
• Clarity and Writing Quality: The manuscript is well organized, with clear motivation, detailed method descriptions, and informative figures illustrating the dataset statistics and model architecture. Algorithms are explained step by step and are easy to follow.  
• Novel Dataset: EAGLE represents a substantial advance in scale and complexity for irregular-mesh fluid simulation benchmarks. With over 1.1 million dynamic meshes sampled at 30 fps and diverse ground profiles (step, linear, spline), it fills an important gap for learning turbulent flows on realistic, moving meshes.  
• Methodological Innovation: The mesh transformer’s combination of offline clustering, learned pooling, and global self-attention is a novel pipeline for fluid forecasting. By reducing attention complexity, it captures long-range spatial interactions more efficiently than iterative message passing.  
• Experimental Thoroughness: The authors provide extensive quantitative results (N-RMSE over multiple time horizons) and qualitative visualizations (flow field rollouts, attention maps aligning with high-velocity regions). They compare against strong baselines (MeshGraphNet, GAT, DilResNet), conduct ablation studies on cluster size and attention heads, and show generalization to unseen geometries and downsampled meshes.  
• Interpretability Analyses: Attention visualizations convincingly demonstrate that the model learns to attend to regions influenced by the moving source. Ablations on clustering granularity and receptive field size support the design choices.

Weaknesses  
• 2D Limitation and Real-World Applicability: Like prior works, the focus on 2D simulations limits immediate applicability to real-world 3D flows. A brief discussion of how the architecture might extend to 3D meshes, or preliminary real-world tests, would strengthen the paper’s impact.  
• Simulator Dependence: The entire benchmark is generated via a high-end fluid simulator. Thus, model performance is inherently tied to simulator fidelity. The paper would benefit from (a) a short discussion of how simulator errors propagate to learning, and (b) an outline of any plans to validate on physical experiments or real datasets.  
• Computational Demands: EAGLE’s generation and the model’s training require substantial compute resources and CFD software. While the authors promise code and data release, clear statements about hardware requirements and training runtimes (possibly in a succinct table) would aid reproducibility.  
• Missing Failure-Case Analysis: Although the paper includes many ablations, it lacks explicit discussion of failure modes or scenarios where the model’s autoregressive rollouts degrade. A dedicated failure analysis or examples of error accumulation over long horizons would be valuable.  
• Deferred Implementation Details: Some architectural hyperparameters (e.g. mesh‐downsampling heuristics, clustering algorithms, detailed solver configurations) are buried in the appendix. Integrating key details into the main text would improve accessibility for readers aiming to reproduce the results.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Add a short paragraph on real-scene applications and possible extensions to 3D, noting any preliminary experiments or theoretical challenges.  
2. Include a failure case discussion or visual examples of rollouts where predictions diverge, to give readers insight into model limitations.  
3. Summarize compute resources (GPU types, runtime per epoch, total training time) and solver cost in the main paper, perhaps as a compact table.  
4. Bring critical implementation details—cluster size selection, mesh‐simplification heuristics—into the main sections rather than relegating them entirely to supplements.  

Decision and Rating  
Overall, this paper makes a solid contribution by releasing a uniquely large, dynamic mesh dataset and introducing a mesh transformer that leverages clustering and global attention to achieve state-of-the-art results. The clarity, methodological innovation, and thorough experiments justify publication. However, constraints such as the 2D scope, reliance on simulated data, and lack of failure‐mode discussion temper the impact. In keeping with a marginally above-threshold recommendation, I rate this submission a 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This manuscript proposed a method to train neural networks to predict turbulent fluid dynamics. For the neural architecture, this manuscript introduced the concept of a transformer into the fluid dynamics field, which outperforms previous methods due to long-range receptive fields. To support the training of the mesh transformer, this manuscript generates a large-scale 2D aerodynamics dataset using a controlled drone. The experiments showed the superior performance of their method over several different baselines.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strength:
- The dataset is large-scale. It covers a big fraction of the interest of the physical datasets. Previous datasets are mostly steady-state simulations.
- The manuscript compared their method with several well-known baselines.

Weaknesses:
- In my opinion, the weaknesses are obvious. First, the dataset only contains 2D data, whose usefulness is suspicious due to the diffusion of fluid. I wonder if the authors can provide more insights on how they are useful in real engineering.
- Several previous datasets are working in CFD, e.g., http://turbulence.pha.jhu.edu/. How is the proposed dataset compared to theirs?
- The only variation in the dataset is the geometry. However, in reality, there are more descriptions of fluid (it is true even for air itself), e.g., the Reynolds number, the temperature, the boundary conditions, and the wind speed. I wonder how generalizable a network trained on this dataset can be.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: The manuscript is well-written. I followed their motivation and method.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The dataset is valuable to the community. However, I would like to see the authors justify my questions about the dataset. I am open to changing the score based on the response.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This manuscript proposed a method to train neural networks to predict turbulent fluid dynamics. For the neural architecture, this manuscript introduced the concept of a transformer into the fluid dynamics field, which outperforms previous methods due to long-range receptive fields. To support the training of the mesh transformer, this manuscript generates a large-scale 2D aerodynamics dataset using a controlled drone. The experiments showed the superior performance of their method over several different baselines.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strength:
- The dataset is large-scale. It covers a big fraction of the interest of the physical datasets. Previous datasets are mostly steady-state simulations.
- The manuscript compared their method with several well-known baselines.

Weaknesses:
- In my opinion, the weaknesses are obvious. First, the dataset only contains 2D data, whose usefulness is suspicious due to the diffusion of fluid. I wonder if the authors can provide more insights on how they are useful in real engineering.
- Several previous datasets are working in CFD, e.g., http://turbulence.pha.jhu.edu/. How is the proposed dataset compared to theirs?
- The only variation in the dataset is the geometry. However, in reality, there are more descriptions of fluid (it is true even for air itself), e.g., the Reynolds number, the temperature, the boundary conditions, and the wind speed. I wonder how generalizable a network trained on this dataset can be.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
The manuscript is well-written. I followed their motivation and method.

Summary Of The Review:
The dataset is valuable to the community. However, I would like to see the authors justify my questions about the dataset. I am open to changing the score based on the response.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents EAGLE, a large‐scale synthetic dataset of approximately 1.1 million time‐varying 2D irregular meshes simulating unsteady, turbulent airflow around a moving UAV over procedurally generated ground geometries. Building on the main contribution, the authors introduce a mesh transformer architecture that reduces attention complexity via offline geometric clustering, learned graph pooling, and multi‐head self‐attention on a coarse set of tokens, thereby capturing long-range dependencies in a single step. Experiments on EAGLE and two standard benchmarks (Cylinder-Flow and Scalar-Flow) show state-of-the-art forecasting accuracy, supported by attention‐map visualizations, extensive ablations on clustering and receptive fields, and generalization studies to unseen geometries and mesh resolutions.

Strengths  
- Dataset scale and dynamism: EAGLE constitutes one of the largest 2D unsteady turbulent flow datasets on irregular meshes to date, filling a gap left by prior steady-state or grid-based CFD benchmarks. Its dynamic remeshing and procedural geometry diversity improve over existing resources such as the JHU turbulence database.  
- Model innovation: The mesh transformer effectively combines k-means clustering, GRU-based pooling, and global self-attention, enabling one-hop long-range receptive fields that outperform both graph neural network and convolutional baselines (e.g., MeshGraphNet, DilResNet).  
- Empirical rigor: The paper presents thorough quantitative results (N-RMSE over multiple rollout horizons), ablations on cluster size, attention scope, and failure cases, as well as qualitative visualizations demonstrating that attention maps align with key flow features.  
- Clarity and organization: The manuscript is well written, with clear motivation, detailed methodological descriptions, and informative figures. Appendices document hyperparameters, training schedules, and supplemental baselines.

Weaknesses  
- Restriction to 2D flows: As noted in both the main and alternative reviews, focusing solely on 2D limits immediate applicability to real-world 3D engineering problems. The paper would benefit from a discussion on extension pathways to three dimensions.  
- Limited physical variation: The dataset’s only source of diversity is geometric; it omits variations in Reynolds number, temperature, boundary conditions, and wind speed. This raises questions about how a network trained exclusively on EAGLE would generalize to differing flow regimes in practice.  
- Dataset comparisons and benchmarks: While EAGLE advances scale and complexity, a more explicit comparison to existing CFD datasets (e.g., JHU turbulence database) and to learned implicit PDE solver approaches would help position its contributions.  
- Computational cost and reproducibility: Generating EAGLE’s million-mesh simulations and training the mesh transformer require substantial compute resources and specialized CFD solvers, which may hinder replication. Although a code and data release is promised, more guidance on hardware requirements or lighter variants would improve accessibility.  
- Deferred implementation details: Some critical engineering choices—mesh simplification heuristics, solver configurations, and hyperparameter settings—are tucked into the appendix. Integrating key details into the main text would enhance reproducibility and reader comprehension.  
- Error accumulation over long rollouts: As highlighted in alternative reviews, the model exhibits growing error in extended autoregressive forecasts. A deeper analysis of this limitation and potential mitigation strategies (e.g., hybrid iterative refinement) would strengthen the evaluation.

Detailed Suggestions  
1. Include a comparative analysis of EAGLE against prominent CFD benchmarks (e.g., JHU turbulence dataset) and learned PDE solvers, both in terms of dataset statistics and model performance.  
2. Discuss strategies for extending the approach to 3D flows, and, if possible, provide preliminary scaling estimates or small-scale 3D experiments.  
3. Expand the dataset’s scope or at least outline plans to incorporate additional physical parameters (Reynolds number variation, temperature, boundary conditions) to assess generalization beyond geometric diversity.  
4. Summarize the most critical implementation and solver parameters in the main manuscript, relegating only secondary details to the appendix.  
5. Provide recommendations for reproducing results with limited hardware (e.g., reduced mesh or model sizes) and clarify the computational budget used for data generation and training.  
6. Analyze the sources of error growth in long-horizon rollouts and discuss potential architectural or training remedies (for instance, periodic recalibration with ground truth).

Decision  
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold.  
This work makes a substantial contribution to learned fluid dynamics through its unprecedented dataset scale and an innovative mesh transformer architecture. At the same time, its current 2D focus, limited physical diversity, and heavy computational demands warrant further justification. I recommend acceptance, contingent on the authors addressing the above concerns to solidify the paper’s impact and reproducibility.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper makes two contributions: First, it presents a large-scale 2D fluid simulation dataset called EAGLE. The dataset consists of 2D scenes with (dynamically changing) rigid boundaries and Reynold-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) fluids. Second, it proposes a neural network model (mesh transformer) for solving fluid problems and compares its performance with popular neural network simulators (MeshGraphNet, etc).
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
The proposal of a large-scale fluid simulation dataset is very timely. I believe having a high-quality dataset will foster relevant research in the learning community.

On the other hand, part of me feels this dataset is still premature. It is probably better than many existing fluid simulation datasets in previous papers, but I think it could have been more polished.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
There are quite a few things unclear to me in the simulation dataset:
- Are there any reasons behind the choice of simulating RANS instead of the original Navier-Stokes equations in the dataset?
- It looks like the drone and the fluid are one-way coupled instead of two-way coupled: the drone provides boundary conditions to the fluid’s motion, but the fluid does not affect the drone’s motion (there are no velocity or pressure terms in Eqn. 8). If the fluid has no influence on the drone, I feel the MPC control is a bit redundant and a kinematic drone should suffice.
- I am not sure I get how the drone’s mesh serves as the boundary condition of the fluid area. It looks like its bounding box is used. Could you elaborate on the velocity/pressure boundary conditions around the bounding boxes? I was expecting some inlet/outlet flow velocity to be set at where the propellers are, but I didn’t find details about it.
- I don’t quite understand the task definition at the end of Sec. 3. Do the node positions x_i change over time? It looks like the fluid area is remeshed at every frame if I am not mistaken.

I initially thought the proposed dataset simulated two-way coupled Navier-Stokes fluids and a dynamic, controllable UAV when reading the main text and was quite excited. It would be good if the paper could clarify the setting upfront in Sec. 3.

Regarding the network model, it looks like the velocity and pressure fields are predicted purely based on the original mesh at the current time followed by resampled to the new mesh if I am not mistaken. This seems questionable to me because I think the correct velocity and pressure field at the next time step will be determined by both the current and new mesh.

Reproducibility: I guess this largely depends on what will be included in the released dataset. Replicating the full simulation results using Ansys is probably intractable for people with limited access to computational resources, but I still suggest the dataset include configurations of the scenes in Ansys if possible.

Other comments:
- Is EAGLE an acronym?
- The baselines are confusing: the beginning of Sec. 5 refers MeshGraphNet and Cylinder-Flow to Li et al. 2019, but I don’t think that paper means either of them. By MeshGraphNet the paper probably means Pfaff et al. [2021].
- I appreciate that the paper frankly talked about failure cases and poor results (Scalar flow) in the experiments.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper makes two contributions: First, it presents a large-scale 2D fluid simulation dataset called EAGLE. The dataset consists of 2D scenes with (dynamically changing) rigid boundaries and Reynold-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) fluids. Second, it proposes a neural network model (mesh transformer) for solving fluid problems and compares its performance with popular neural network simulators (MeshGraphNet, etc).

Strength And Weaknesses:
The proposal of a large-scale fluid simulation dataset is very timely. I believe having a high-quality dataset will foster relevant research in the learning community.

On the other hand, part of me feels this dataset is still premature. It is probably better than many existing fluid simulation datasets in previous papers, but I think it could have been more polished.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
There are quite a few things unclear to me in the simulation dataset:
- Are there any reasons behind the choice of simulating RANS instead of the original Navier-Stokes equations in the dataset?
- It looks like the drone and the fluid are one-way coupled instead of two-way coupled: the drone provides boundary conditions to the fluid’s motion, but the fluid does not affect the drone’s motion (there are no velocity or pressure terms in Eqn. 8). If the fluid has no influence on the drone, I feel the MPC control is a bit redundant and a kinematic drone should suffice.
- I am not sure I get how the drone’s mesh serves as the boundary condition of the fluid area. It looks like its bounding box is used. Could you elaborate on the velocity/pressure boundary conditions around the bounding boxes? I was expecting some inlet/outlet flow velocity to be set at where the propellers are, but I didn’t find details about it.
- I don’t quite understand the task definition at the end of Sec. 3. Do the node positions x_i change over time? It looks like the fluid area is remeshed at every frame if I am not mistaken.

I initially thought the proposed dataset simulated two-way coupled Navier-Stokes fluids and a dynamic, controllable UAV when reading the main text and was quite excited. It would be good if the paper could clarify the setting upfront in Sec. 3.

Regarding the network model, it looks like the velocity and pressure fields are predicted purely based on the original mesh at the current time followed by resampled to the new mesh if I am not mistaken. This seems questionable to me because I think the correct velocity and pressure field at the next time step will be determined by both the current and new mesh.

Reproducibility: I guess this largely depends on what will be included in the released dataset. Replicating the full simulation results using Ansys is probably intractable for people with limited access to computational resources, but I still suggest the dataset include configurations of the scenes in Ansys if possible.

Other comments:
- Is EAGLE an acronym?
- The baselines are confusing: the beginning of Sec. 5 refers MeshGraphNet and Cylinder-Flow to Li et al. 2019, but I don’t think that paper means either of them. By MeshGraphNet the paper probably means Pfaff et al. [2021].
- I appreciate that the paper frankly talked about failure cases and poor results (Scalar flow) in the experiments.

Summary Of The Review:
Currently, I am leaning toward rejection, although my score is not final yet. It is definitely a respectable submission, and I welcome different opinions from the authors and other reviewers.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents two main contributions. First, it introduces EAGLE, a large‐scale synthetic dataset of approximately 1.1 million time‐varying 2D irregular meshes simulating unsteady, turbulent airflow around a moving UAV interacting with procedurally generated ground geometries. Scenes encompass 600 distinct profiles (step, linear, spline), sampled at 30 fps over 33 s, with dynamic remeshing to track the UAV’s motion. Second, the authors propose a mesh transformer architecture that reduces spatial complexity via offline geometric clustering and learned graph pooling, applies multi‐head self‐attention on the coarsened token graph to capture long-range dependencies in one hop, and decodes back to the original mesh through a GNN decoder. The model is benchmarked against strong baselines (MeshGraphNet, Cylinder‐Flow, Scalar‐Flow, GAT, DilResNet), achieving state‐of‐the‐art forecasting accuracy, supported by extensive ablations on clustering, receptive fields, and generalization to unseen geometries and mesh resolutions, as well as attention visualizations correlating with airflow patterns.

Strengths  
1. Dataset scale and diversity: EAGLE substantially advances existing benchmarks by providing over a million frames of dynamic, irregular‐mesh fluid simulations. Its combination of UAV‐induced turbulence and procedurally generated boundary geometries fills a critical gap for learning-based fluid dynamics on realistic meshes.  
2. Model innovation: The mesh transformer’s pipeline—offline k-means clustering, GRU-based pooling, global self‐attention on coarse tokens, and GNN decoding—efficiently captures long-range interactions without multi-hop message passing. Attention maps align intuitively with flow features, and ablations on cluster size and attention scope convincingly support the design choices.  
3. Empirical evaluation: The experiments are comprehensive, spanning EAGLE and two established benchmarks (Cylinder-Flow, Scalar-Flow). Quantitative metrics (e.g., N-RMSE), qualitative rollouts, failure‐case analyses, and generalization studies to novel geometries and downsampled meshes all reinforce the method’s practical value.  
4. Interpretability and ablations: Visualization of attention weights shows meaningful correlations with airflow structures. Ablations on clustering strategies, number of heads, and receptive fields provide clear insights into each component’s contribution.

Weaknesses and Suggestions  
1. Simulation assumptions and clarity:  
   • RANS vs. Navier–Stokes. The rationale for using Reynold‐Averaged Navier–Stokes rather than the full Navier–Stokes equations is not discussed. Clarifying this choice in Sec. 3 would help readers assess physical fidelity.  
   • One-way coupling. The drone serves only as a boundary condition, with no feedback from fluid forces in Eqn. 8. This raises the question of whether MPC control and dynamic UAV motion are necessary; a kinematic trajectory might suffice. The authors should justify the decoupling or extend to two-way coupling.  
   • Boundary conditions. It is unclear how the UAV mesh imposes velocity/pressure boundaries—does the bounding box substitute for detailed inlet/outlet flow around propellers? More explicit description of boundary condition setup (e.g., local inflow regions) is needed.  
   • Remeshing and task definition. The node positions appear to change each frame, implying remeshing at every time step. The paper should clarify whether forecasts predict fields on a fixed reference mesh or dynamically remeshed domains.  

2. Scope and applicability:  
   • 2D limitation. While the 2D setting enables large-scale data generation, real-world fluid dynamics is inherently 3D. A discussion of how the approach might extend to 3D meshes—or the obstacles thereto—would be valuable.  
   • Fixed UAV trajectory. All simulations follow canonical flight paths; adding diversity in motion patterns could strengthen the dataset’s representativeness.  
   • Computational cost. Generating the dataset with a commercial CFD solver (Ansys) and training the mesh transformer requires substantial resources. The paper should detail hardware requirements, solver configurations, and any mesh-downsampling heuristics to guide readers with limited compute.

3. Reproducibility and documentation:  
   • Dataset release. To facilitate replication, the authors should include full scene configurations, solver parameters, and remeshing scripts.  
   • Code and hyperparameters. While appendices list many training details, some critical choices (e.g., cluster numbers, attention head counts, learning schedules) are scattered. A consolidated reproducibility checklist or code repository with scripts will greatly aid adoption.  
   • Baseline references. The current text confuses MeshGraphNet and Cylinder‐Flow references (Li et al. 2019 vs. Pfaff et al. 2021). Ensuring accurate citations and clarifying each baseline’s origin would improve scholarly rigor.  
   • Acronym clarification. If EAGLE is an acronym, its expansion should be provided early in the manuscript.

Final Recommendation  
This submission offers a compelling dataset and a novel mesh transformer architecture, backed by thorough experiments and insightful analyses. However, key aspects of the physical simulation setup, boundary conditions, and task formulation remain under-specified, and the heavy computational demands may impede broader reproducibility. These clarity and scope limitations currently outweigh the paper’s significant contributions. I therefore recommend marginal rejection (Rating: 5: marginally below the acceptance threshold) while encouraging the authors to address the above concerns in a revision.

title: Consolidator: Mergable Adapter with Group Connections for Visual Adaptation

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This work proposes a consolidator that modifies the pretrained model with the addition of a small set of tunable parameters to store the task-specific knowledge while freezing the backbone model during adaptation. A two-stage consolidation process is designed by merging corresponding parameters in the training-storage phase and loading-inference phase. Extensive experiments are conducted on various downstream tasks and the results outperform state-of-the-art methods with fewer stored parameters but superior performance.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strength:

(1) The authors propose a basic module, a consolidator for efficient transfer learning.

(2) A two-stage consolidation process is designed. 

(3) Extensive experiments on various downstream tasks show state-of-the-art performance.

Weaknesses:
This work aims to design a mergeable adapter for efficient transfer learning and proposes a two-stage consolidation process by merging corresponding parameters. I have some questions as follows. 

(1)  In Figure 1, the authors split channels and use zero padding, which might cause a heavy computational cost. Thus, the training time/speed should be shown for comparison with other tuning methods. Besides, I notice that the authors insert a consolidator in the FC layer in Figure 1. Will performance be further enhanced if the consolidator is inserted in other locations? Some recent tuning methods [1, 2] should also be discussed and compared in Table 1. 

(2)  Some other tuning baselines are missing in Table 4. The authors should conduct more experiments with other tuning methods for fair comparisons. 

(3)  The consolidator is designed for a linear layer. Can it be applied to other operational structures, such as self attention. Further, the current consolidator module is designed mainly in the vision transformer. In Table 1, Table 4 and Table 5, the proposed method obtains promising performance compared to the Full tuning. Push it forward, can it be extended to other model families such as CNNs or MLP architectures [3,4,5]? These will be interesting experiments. Due to the rebuttal time constraints, the authors do not need to add these extension experiments, but feel free to discuss them. 

(4)  There exists a large number of typos in the current version, the authors are able to polish it further. 

[1] ‘AdaptFormer: Adapting Vision Transformers for Scalable Visual Recognition’, in NeurIPS2022. 

[2] ‘Scaling & Shifting Your Features: A New Baseline for Efficient Model Tuning’, in NeurIPS2022. 

[3] ‘A ConvNet for the 2020s’, in CVPR2022.

[4] ‘AS-MLP: An Axial Shifted MLP Architecture for Vision’, in ICLR2022.

[5] ‘RepMLPNet: Hierarchical Vision MLP with Re-parameterized Locality’, in CVPR2022.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: It should not be hard to reproduce this work because the authors describe the specific details for training and hyper parameters. The authors also propose consolidation process, which is also original in efficient transfer learning.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This work proposes a two-stage consolidation process for efficient transfer learning. It outperforms state-of-the-art methods with fewer stored parameters but superior performance. I tend to vote marginally above the acceptance threshold. Some additional experiments should be conducted to further improve the quality of this manuscript.

8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper  
This paper introduces Consolidator, a mergeable adapter module for parameter‐ and inference‐efficient transfer learning of vision transformers. The core idea is to attach small multi‐branch grouped connected (GC) layers—augmented with channel reordering and DropPath regularization—to each fully connected layer in a frozen pre‐trained transformer. After task‐specific tuning, duplicate entries in these GC branches and the original bias are merged into a single sparse matrix for compact storage (“training–storage consolidation”), and at inference time this sparse update is fused back into the backbone weights, incurring zero extra computation (“loading–inference consolidation”). Extensive experiments on 19 VTAB‐1k tasks, ten full‐data classification datasets, object detection, semantic segmentation, and self‐supervised ViT backbones demonstrate that Consolidator outperforms full fine‐tuning and state‐of‐the‐art parameter‐efficient methods (Adapter, LoRA, NOAH, SSF, VPT) using as little as 0.35% of parameters, with gains up to +7.56% on VTAB.

Strengths  
1. Novel Mergeable Adapter Design  
   • The multi-branch GC layer with reversible channel reordering and DropPath combines structured sparsity and stochastic depth in a fresh way.  
   • The two‐stage consolidation scheme (training–storage and loading–inference) is well motivated and achieves zero inference overhead.  

2. Comprehensive Empirical Evaluation  
   • Rigorous experiments across diverse settings—VTAB-1k, data-rich classification, object detection, segmentation, supervised and self‐supervised backbones—show consistent superiority over full fine‐tuning and parameter-efficient baselines.  
   • Detailed ablations isolate the contributions of grouping granularity, channel reorder, DropPath versus dropout, and consolidation steps.  

3. Practical Impact  
   • By requiring only 0.3–1.8% extra parameters per task and no extra runtime cost, Consolidator addresses the pressing trade‐off between adaptation performance, storage footprint, and inference efficiency, making it attractive for resource‐constrained deployments.  

Weaknesses and Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Computational Cost and Baselines  
   • While the paper claims efficient adaptation, the split‐channel and zero‐padding operations in GC layers may introduce overhead. Reporting training time, memory footprint, and throughput comparisons against other tuning methods (e.g., AdaptFormer [1], SSF [2]) would strengthen the empirical case.  
   • Table 1 and Table 4 currently omit several recent baselines (AdaptFormer, Scaling & Shifting Your Features, etc.). Including these methods would ensure a fair and up‐to‐date comparison.  

2. Hyperparameter Complexity and Group Divisibility  
   • The requirement that group sizes exactly divide channel dimensions limits granularity under tight budgets. A deeper theoretical discussion or heuristic guidelines for choosing group size, number of branches, and DropPath rate would help practitioners.  
   • The ablations reveal sensitivity to branch count and group configurations, but more practical recommendations—perhaps via a small hyperparameter‐search protocol—would improve usability.  

3. Architectural Generality  
   • The consolidator is demonstrated primarily on linear (FC) layers in vision transformers. It would be valuable to discuss or preliminarily evaluate its extension to other operations (e.g., self‐attention, convolutional layers) and non‐transformer backbones (CNNs, MLP architectures [3–5]). Such discussion can guide future work even if experiments are beyond the current scope.  
   • Similarly, exploring the effect of inserting consolidators at different network locations (beyond the FC layer) might reveal further performance gains.  

4. Clarity and Presentation  
   • The mathematical notation in some equations contains PDF artifacts (e.g., “cid:80”), and the channel-reorder inverse operation is described but could benefit from additional pseudocode or a more detailed diagram.  
   • A concise summary table of all hyperparameters (group sizes, branch counts, DropPath rates) in the main text would aid reproduction. The appendix is thorough but can be overwhelming without a high-level overview.  
   • A round of careful proofreading to eliminate typos and notation inconsistencies will improve readability.  

Clarity, Novelty, and Reproducibility  
   • Clarity: The overall flow—from motivation through methodology to experiments—is logical and well structured. Key components (GC layer, ChannelReorder, DropPath, two‐stage consolidation) are described with sufficient detail, though some notation fixes and pseudocode would help.  
   • Novelty: The structured multi‐branch GC adapter combined with a dual consolidation scheme represents a significant advance over existing adapters, LoRA, and prompt‐tuning approaches in uniting storage efficiency, inference neutrality, and adaptation performance.  
   • Reproducibility: Hyperparameter settings, dataset splits, and training protocols are detailed in appendices, and a code release is promised. While the number of tuning parameters is non‐trivial, the provided experimental recipes and public code should allow practitioners to validate and build on this work.

Overall Recommendation  
This paper presents a well‐motivated, technically sound, and thoroughly evaluated method for parameter‐ and inference‐efficient adaptation of vision transformers. Despite minor concerns around computational profiling, hyperparameter complexity, and presentation polish, the methodological contributions and state‐of‐the‐art empirical results are compelling. I therefore recommend acceptance.

Rating: 8: accept, good paper

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This work proposed **consolidator** to efficiently fine-tune transformers. Consolidator has multi-branches structures, which can be merged into a single matrix and saved on the disk. Extensive experiments demonstrate the efficiency and effectiveness of consolidator on various vision tasks.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
#### Strength

1. The design of the consolidator is parameter-efficient since its mergeable property. By extending a single branch to multiple branches, the consolidator has better flexibility and transferability.

2.  The two-stage process maximizes the adaptation capacity without extra inference costs.

3. The paper is well-written and easy to follow.

#### Weaknesses

1. The novelty of this work is limited. The mergeable design is proposed by LoRA [1] for efficient parameter finetuing. Besides, what's the main difference between the **Channel Reorder** and **shuffle** operator in ShuffleNet[2]?

2. Some recent related works are not discussed, e.g. [3][4][5].

3. The improvement is marginal compared with NOAH in Table 1.


[1] Hu, Edward J., et al. "Lora: Low-rank adaptation of large language models." arXiv 2021.

[2] Zhang, Xiangyu, et al. "Shufflenet: An extremely efficient convolutional neural network for mobile devices." CVPR 2018.

[3] Bahng, Hyojin, et al. "Exploring visual prompts for adapting large-scale models." arXiv 2022.

[4] Chen, Shoufa, et al. "AdaptFormer: Adapting Vision Transformers for Scalable Visual Recognition." NeurIPS 2022.

[5] Jie, Shibo, and Zhi-Hong Deng. "Convolutional bypasses are better vision transformer adapters." arXiv 2022.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
The paper is clearly written and easy to follow.

A typo in Sec 3.2: **aim ti**.

There is no source code available so I can not evaluate the reproducibility currently.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: See above.

[2] Zhang, Xiangyu, et al. "Shufflenet: An extremely efficient convolutional neural network for mobile devices." CVPR 2018.

[3] Bahng, Hyojin, et al. "Exploring visual prompts for adapting large-scale models." arXiv 2022.

[4] Chen, Shoufa, et al. "AdaptFormer: Adapting Vision Transformers for Scalable Visual Recognition." NeurIPS 2022.

[5] Jie, Shibo, and Zhi-Hong Deng. "Convolutional bypasses are better vision transformer adapters." arXiv 2022.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
The paper is clearly written and easy to follow.

A typo in Sec 3.2: **aim ti**.

There is no source code available so I can not evaluate the reproducibility currently.

Summary Of The Review:
See above.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper  
This paper proposes “Consolidator,” a mergeable adapter for parameter- and inference-efficient fine-tuning of vision transformers. Consolidator attaches lightweight, grouped-connected (GC) branches—augmented with channel reordering and DropPath regularization—to each fully connected layer in a frozen backbone. After task-specific tuning, duplicate parameters across branches are first consolidated into a sparse matrix for compact storage, then merged back into the original weights at load time, yielding zero extra inference cost. The authors evaluate across a broad suite of benchmarks—including VTAB-1k, ten full-data image‐classification datasets, self-supervised ViTs, object detection, and semantic segmentation—and report consistent gains over full fine-tuning and state-of-the-art adapter methods (Adapter, LoRA, NOAH, SSF) while using as little as 0.3–0.5% additional parameters per task.

Strengths  
1. Parameter and Inference Efficiency: By design, the two-stage consolidation achieves storage savings and inference neutrality, addressing a key trade-off in deploying large vision models on resource-constrained devices.  
2. Empirical Scope and Performance: The paper presents extensive experiments—spanning 19 VTAB tasks, ten full-data classification sets, and downstream detection/segmentation—demonstrating that Consolidator consistently outperforms baselines (up to +7.56% on VTAB) with only 0.35% extra parameters. Ablation studies further isolate the contributions of grouped connections, channel reorder, and DropPath.  
3. Clear Motivation and Presentation: The overall narrative is logical and well organized; figures and tables comprehensively summarize results. The two-stage consolidation concept is intuitively presented, and experimental protocols (e.g., group sizes, branch counts) are described in detail.

Weaknesses  
1. Novelty and Relation to Prior Work: The core idea of a mergeable, low-parameter adapter parallels LoRA’s low-rank adaptation, and the channel reorder bears resemblance to ShuffleNet’s shuffle operator. The paper does not clearly articulate the primary distinctions from these predecessors, nor does it sufficiently discuss other recent works such as Visual Prompts [Bahng et al. 2022], AdaptFormer [Chen et al. 2022], or convolutional bypass adapters [Jie and Deng 2022].  
2. Marginal Gains Over Strong Baselines: In Table 1, the improvement over NOAH is modest, raising questions about whether the added complexity truly justifies the benefits in all settings.  
3. Theoretical and Practical Clarifications: While the empirical results are robust, the paper lacks a formal analysis of trade-offs between group granularity and adaptation capacity. Moreover, group-size and divisibility constraints can limit fine-grained storage budgets, and practical guidelines for hyperparameter tuning (group counts, drop rates) remain heuristic.  
4. Clarity and Reproducibility Concerns: Although generally well written, the notation is sometimes dense—PDF artifacts (e.g., “(cid:80)”) appear in equations—and certain algorithmic steps (e.g., the inverse channel reorder) would benefit from explicit pseudocode or diagrams. Crucially, no source code is presently available, hindering exact reproduction of the results and verification of hardware-level time/memory profiles.

Clarity, Quality, Novelty, and Reproducibility  
The methodological exposition—particularly the mathematical formulations of grouped connected layers and the consolidation phases—is comprehensive, yet readers may struggle with the dense notation and occasional formatting typos (e.g., “aim ti” in Sec 3.2). The empirical evaluation is among the most thorough in the adapter literature, but without code release, reproducing the intricate hyperparameter setup and verifying performance on additional real-world multitask or latency benchmarks will be challenging. While the multi-branch GC adapter with reversible channel reorder and dual consolidation stages represents a creative union of structured sparsity and mergeability, the paper would be strengthened by deeper theoretical justification, clearer differentiation from closely related methods, and practical recipes for hyperparameter selection.

Recommendation  
Rating: 5 (marginally below the acceptance threshold)  
Consolidator is a well-engineered and empirically strong approach to parameter-efficient vision-transformer adaptation with zero inference overhead. However, its limited novelty relative to existing mergeable adapters and channel-shuffle techniques, coupled with marginal gains over top baselines, intricate hyperparameter dependencies, and current reproducibility barriers, place it just below the bar for acceptance in its present form.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper presents a new fine-tuning method for vision transformers that only trains and stores very few parameters. The fine-tuning method achieves both efficiency and effectiveness through its design of mergeable multiple branches and parameter shifting. Extensive experiments show the method surpasses other adapter methods and full fine-tuning on several transfer learning tasks.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strength:
1. Consolidator is a very effective method. Compared with other adapter methods and full parameter fine-tuning methods, Consolidator can achieve an almost optimal transfer learning effect.
2. Compared to the adapter methods, the design in this paper can be merged with the original model so that the inference phase does not increase the cost of memory and computation time.

Weakness:
1. The prior of ChannelReorder operation is strong but the explanation of its effectiveness is weak. 
2. The parameters in all tables are stored parameters for Consolidator but not trainable parameters.
3. It should be possible to save time compared to the full parameter finetune during training, I wonder why this is not reported, nor is it compared to other baseline training time.
4. The value of saving storage space is not so great, especially for the base-level model, and there are few experiments on the Large model.
5. There are slightly more spelling errors and grammatical errors. P3 workss; P4 ti, thw; P5 the all the; P7 accomodate.

reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper proposes Consolidator, a novel parameter-efficient fine-tuning method for vision transformers. By attaching small, grouped connected (GC) branches with channel reordering and DropPath regularization to each fully connected layer of a frozen ViT, Consolidator learns only a tiny fraction of task-specific parameters (as low as 0.35%) while incurring zero extra inference cost. A two-stage consolidation process first merges duplicate branch parameters into a sparse matrix for compact storage, then injects them back into the backbone weights at load time. Extensive experiments on VTAB-1k (19 tasks), ten full-data classification datasets, self-supervised ViT, object detection, and segmentation demonstrate that Consolidator consistently outperforms full fine-tuning and state-of-the-art adapters (Adapter, LoRA, NOAH, SSF), achieving up to +7.56% accuracy gains with minimal overhead.

Strengths  
1. Effectiveness and Efficiency: Consolidator matches or surpasses full fine-tuning and existing adapter methods across diverse benchmarks, while requiring only 0.3–1.8% additional parameters and no extra inference cost.  
2. Mergeable Design: The two-stage consolidation (training-storage and loading-inference) is well motivated and practically valuable, eliminating memory and runtime overhead during deployment.  
3. Comprehensive Evaluation: The authors evaluate on a broad spectrum of tasks and backbones (supervised/self-supervised ViT, Swin, ConvNeXt, AS-MLP), with thorough ablations isolating the contributions of grouping, channel reorder, DropPath, and consolidation stages.  
4. Reproducibility: Detailed methodological descriptions, ablation tables, and promised code release support reproducibility. Pseudocode for the GC layer and consolidation scheme is largely clear, and appendix materials specify hyperparameters and dataset splits.

Weaknesses  
1. Theoretical Justification: The strong structural prior introduced by the ChannelReorder operation lacks a formal explanation. A deeper theoretical or empirical analysis of why channel reordering consistently boosts transfer performance would strengthen the contribution.  
2. Training-Time Comparison: While inference efficiency is well demonstrated, the paper omits training-time and memory profiling versus full fine-tuning and other adapters. Reporting wall-clock and GPU-memory costs during training would clarify the overall efficiency trade-offs.  
3. Storage Savings Scope: The net storage reduction is modest for base-sized models, and only a handful of experiments are reported on large (e.g., ViT-Large) architectures. Additional large-model results would substantiate the method’s scalability.  
4. Hyperparameter Complexity: Consolidator introduces multiple tuning knobs (group sizes, branch counts, drop rates) and requires that group sizes divide channel dimensions exactly, limiting finer-grained budgets. Providing heuristic guidelines or an automated search strategy would improve usability.  
5. Clarity and Presentation: Minor formatting artifacts (e.g., “cid:80” in equations) and leftover typos (P3 “workss”; P7 “accomodate”) occasionally disrupt readability. Key algorithmic steps—particularly the inverse channel reorder and sparse merging—would benefit from more concise pseudocode or illustrative diagrams.  
6. Broader Applicability: While the focus on vision transformers is well justified, initial tests on CNN or MLP backbones are limited. Demonstrating that the mergeable adapter framework extends beyond transformers would underscore its generality.

Detailed Comments and Suggestions  
• ChannelReorder Rationale: Include an ablation that compares random versus learned versus fixed reorder patterns, and discuss the interplay with grouped connections. A short theorem or intuitive argument on capacity expansion through channel shuffling could be illuminating.  
• Training Profiling: Add a table or plot comparing training epochs, GPU-hours, and peak memory use across Consolidator, full fine-tuning, and representative adapter baselines.  
• Large-Scale Experiments: Report Consolidator performance on ViT-Large or other large backbones to confirm that the storage and accuracy benefits persist at scale.  
• Hyperparameter Guidelines: Summarize recommended group sizes, branch counts, and drop rates for different budget regimes in a single table. Consider providing a simple auto-tuning recipe (e.g., grid search ranges) in the appendix.  
• Clarity Enhancements: Clean up notation artifacts, fix spelling errors, and consolidate the extensive appendix into a short hyperparameter summary. Supplement the text with a flowchart detailing the two-stage consolidation.  
• Multi-Task and Latency Benchmarks: If space permits, include a real-world multi-task or latency benchmark (e.g., on-device inference) to showcase how zero-overhead consolidation translates to deployment gains.

Decision  
Rating: 5 – marginally below the acceptance threshold.  
Consolidator represents a technically sound and empirically strong step forward in mergeable, parameter-efficient adapters for vision transformers. However, to meet the bar for acceptance, the paper needs deeper theoretical insights into key design choices, full training-time profiling, expanded large-model experiments, and improved clarity around hyperparameter selection and notation.

reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents a mergeable adapter framework—termed Consolidator—for efficient fine-tuning of vision transformers on resource-limited devices. Building on grouped connections with re-parameterization, channel reorder transformations, and DropPath regularization, the method attaches lightweight multi-branch GC modules to each fully connected layer of a frozen backbone. After task-specific tuning, a two-stage consolidation first merges duplicate GC parameters into a sparse storage format and then fuses those sparse updates back into the original weights at load time, incurring zero additional inference cost. Experiments on VTAB-1k, ten full-data classification benchmarks, object detection, segmentation, and self-supervised ViT backbones report up to +7.5% gains over full fine-tuning and state-of-the-art parameter-efficient methods (Adapter, LoRA, NOAH, SSF) using as little as 0.35% extra parameters.

Strengths  
• Novel two-stage consolidation: The merge-and-store workflow effectively eliminates inference overhead while achieving structured sparsity, unifying ideas from prior adapters and low-rank updates.  
• Empirical rigor: Extensive benchmarks across 19 VTAB tasks, ten full-data datasets, and downstream detection/segmentation validate robustness and generality. Ablations isolate the contributions of group size, channel reorder, and DropPath.  
• Storage-efficient adaptation: By exploiting mergeability, the method dramatically reduces on-device storage costs without sacrificing accuracy, addressing an important trade-off in deploying large models.  
• Clear positioning: The paper situates itself relative to Adapter, LoRA, VPT, NOAH, and SSF, and provides detailed mathematical formulations and implementation details (with code release planned) that largely support reproducibility.

Weaknesses  
• Restricted application scenario: The premise—that devices can perform training but lack storage for a full parameter copy—appears unrealistic. Devices capable of fine-tuning typically have sufficient flash or SSD capacity to store model parameters, weakening the stated practical motivation.  
• Transformer relevance vs. re-parameterization: Although framed as adaptation for vision transformers, the core novelty lies in re-parameterization and structured sparsity techniques rather than Transformer-specific insights.  
• Missing recent literature: Key works on lightweight Transformer adaptation (e.g., AdaptFormer: NeurIPS 2022) are not cited, overlooking closely related advances in scalable ViT transfer learning.  
• Hyperparameter complexity: Choosing group sizes that exactly divide channel dimensions, branch counts, and DropPath rates adds considerable tuning overhead, and the paper lacks automated or theoretically justified guidelines for these selections.  
• Clarity and notation issues: Equations include PDF artifacts (e.g., “(cid:80)”), and certain steps—such as the inverse channel reorder and consolidation pseudocode—are under-specified, which may impede reproducibility despite the code release.

Clarity, Quality, Novelty, and Reproducibility  
Overall, the manuscript is well organized, with a logical flow from motivation to methodology to experiments. The mathematical description of GC layers, channel reordering, and the two-stage consolidation scheme is sound, and the extensive appendices supply implementation details. However, the dense notation and minor typographical errors detract from readability. The empirical evaluation is thorough and convincingly demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed adapter, but theoretical analysis of trade-offs (e.g., group granularity versus capacity) and comprehensive latency profiling on real hardware would further strengthen the contribution. Code release will aid reproducibility, although practitioners may still face hurdles tuning numerous new hyperparameters.

Conclusion  
Consolidator offers an inventive mergeable adapter design that achieves state-of-the-art parameter-efficient tuning for vision transformers with zero inference overhead and strong empirical gains. Nevertheless, the contrived application scenario, Transformer-specific novelty concerns, omitted related work, and hyperparameter complexity limit its practical impact. I therefore recommend a marginal rejection.

title: Equal Improvability: A New Fairness Notion Considering the Long-term Impact

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This work proposes a notion of long-term impact called equal improvability (EI) that equalizes the "effort required to improve" of rejected individuals belonging to different sensitive groups. This metric is theoretically compared to three other long-term metrics: Bounded Effort and Equal Recourse---conditions for meeting each of these notions of fairness (in relation to each other) are derived. Three fairness-based regularization methods are proposed to find policies that mitigate unfairness wrt EI, each with a unique differentiable penalty term. These methods are empirically tested against synthetic and real-world data. In the first set of experiments, the three mitigation strategies are tested against a fairness-unaware algorithm over two real-world datasets (non-dynamic). In the second set of experiments, results for a simple dynamical setting are shown.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strengths / comments: 
- I enjoyed reading the introduction and the (first) related work section, where the authors pinpoint the importance of their metric and show how it is distinct from current known metrics in the field. 
- Concepts are elaborated on in the appendix or figures are provided to help readers better understand material. The example of $\mu(\Delta \mathbf{x_1})$ in the para underneath Defn 2.1 was very helpful. 
- The authors did not over-state the results of their fairness-based regularization algorithms, e.g., acknowledged that these methods can mitigate EI unfairness and do not provide further guarantees on solutions found. 
- The organization of section 3 was straightforward and well-written. 

Weaknesses / comments: 

- Why don't the authors compare the other long-term fairness metrics to the long-term fairness-unaware metrics in the first set of experiments? ERM is a very simple baseline, and arguably the three datasets provided are three more datasets over which other long-term fairness baselines can be used as a comparison. We could consider this a single-step episode, which is a subset of an n-step episode. The paper that presents ER also presents a fairness-based regularization method to find solutions that mitigate ER. 

- How easy is it to find policies that mitigate unfairness when there are multiple groups, as is the case in many real-world applications? (For example, in applications where one might consider race, gender, or relevant proxies.)  One of the issues with just adding a (fairness-based) penalty to the objective is that the alg is only given incentive to satisfy fairness, but has the freedom to violate fairness if that results in a more improvement to the original objective. Qualitatively, it seems that scaling the importance of each of the additional pair-wise penalties wrt the primary objective would not be simple or insightful for users. 

- Adding on to the previous point, the Zafar paper that inspired the covariance-based EI penalty creates proxies to the original fairness constraint, but does not show (theoretically) that solutions found by the new proxy-objective solve the original objective. The covariance-based EI method also has no guarantees on the solution found by the algorithms (perhaps the KDE-based penalty as well though I am unfamiliar with the work which inspired it, and did not take the time to read through the work). Ultimately this is a weakness of using fairness-based regularization to mitigate unfairness. I suggest that the users address this as a weakness in the main body.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
Clarity: Overall the paper is straightforward to read. The proofs I skimmed (app A) seem clear. 
- Page 6 in experimental setting: Where are the "statistics for five trials"? Are the errors standard error or standard deviation? 

Reproducibility: Information needed for reproducibility is located in the appendix. I did not look at the code but the authors also assert that hyperparameters used in the experiments are located there as well. I feel confident that I could re-construct the experiments from the paper.

Quality/Novelty: The authors show that the notion of fairness considered is similar to current long-term metrics but distinct, and provide three algorithms, two of which are small changes from previous works that use soft constraints, i.e., create a fairness-based regularization penalty. These methods do not come with guarantees on the solution found by the optimization process, but can be considered stepping stones for future work.  

Small typos / issues and suggested improvements: 
- Appendix B first sentence: "we explain what each term means in EI definition means" -> "we explain what each term in the EI definition means" 
- I did not realize until the end of the paper that there was a second related work section---I suggest mentioning this in the first related work section! 
- "First-order" is never truly defined, and the reader is left to infer from the text. Does first-order imply that these metrics "take potential follow-up inequity risk" one "time-step" into the future, i.e., one decision into the future? The experimental section shows a one-step scenario (the first set of experiments), and a multi-step scenario (three rounds). Are the long-term metrics mentioned actually n-step fairness notions? 
- EI disparity can be inferred from the main text but I suggest that to reduce confusion it is defined in the main work. ATM the authors refer to appendix B.3 and then use this term later numerous times in the main body.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
I make a recommendation to accept. 

- The approach seems well-motivated, and the authors take care to show that their metric is distinct from other current methods in the longterm fairness space. 
- The paper also does not over-state its claims, e.g., the three optimization algorithms introduced offer no guarantees on the solutions found will actually mitigate EI unfairness. This limitation is well-known in regards to soft constraints but is still important to address in the main body (see weaknesses for an elaboration). 
- There are also a few clarifying points from the experiments I would like addressed, such as the choice of baselines for the non-dynamic experiments, as well as the statistics being presented in the experiments. 

--- post rebuttal --- 
I thank the authors for the detailed response! The authors agreed to address many of my concerns in their next iteration, which I think will make the work more straightforward. After discussion with other reviewers, I still lean towards acceptance.
